content.problemStatement:
Anakin's spacecraft is being attacked by Buzz droids. Again. Buzz droids intend to disable rather than destroy their targets. They achieve this by introducing bugs in the program of the underlying software.



One important module of every navigation controller is the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. Here is how the code for Floyd-Warshall looks like:


`
n <- number of vertices in the graph

adj[n][n] <- adjacency matrix(0/1) of a simple undirected graph

connected[n][n] <- 2D matrix of size n * n

for (i=1; i<=n; i++)
  for (j=1; j<=n; j++)
    connected[i][j] = adj[i][j]

for (p=1; p<=n; p++)    // runs only up to n-k after reprogramming
  for (i=1; i<=n; i++)
    for (j=1; j<=n; j++)
      connected[i][j] = (connected[i][j]) 
			|| (i!=j && connected[i][p] && connected[p][j])

return connected
`

Due to reprogramming by Buzz droids, now the outer loop on **p** runs only from **1** to **n-k** (both inclusive).


Your task is to find the number of simple undirected unweighted graphs for which the reprogrammed code still returns the correct answer. Two graphs are considered different if their adjacency matrices are not identical.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers, n and k, on a single line.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of graphs (modulo 10^9+7) for which the buggy code returns the correct answer.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one possible graph with one vertex. For the second test case, there are two possible graphs with two vertices. For the third test case, there are seven possible graphs with three vertices.
For the first test case, there is only one possible graph with one vertex. For the second test case, there is only one possible graph with three vertices.
For this test case, there is only one possible graph with four vertices.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by analyzing the conditions under which the reprogrammed code returns the correct answer. This involves understanding the properties of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm and how the reprogramming affects its output. The key insight is to identify the conditions under which the reprogrammed code produces the same result as the original Floyd-Warshall algorithm.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) per test case, O(T * n) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space complexity is constant as we only use a fixed amount of space to store the input and output.
